steps.title: You Crossed the Threshold
steps.body:
Week 1 was diagnosis. You looked at your marriage with honest eyes — the cracks, the strengths, the patterns you'd been too busy to notice.

Week 2 is different. This is Repair Week. This is where knowledge becomes action.

Here's the insight most couples miss: knowing what's broken isn't enough. You need a system for fixing it.

A doctor doesn't just tell you you're sick — they give you a treatment plan. A mechanic doesn't just point at the engine problem — they fix it.

You've had your diagnosis. Now you get the repair manual.

This week, you'll learn the specific skills that turn damaged walls into bridges.

Diagnosis gives you the truth.
Repair gives you the tools.

The first week was about seeing.
This week is about doing.

Don't just talk about what needs to heal.
Pick up a tool and start mending.

questions.label:
What's the single most important thing Week 1 revealed about your marriage?

Now answer this: what would change if the two of you spent this week actively working on that one thing instead of just knowing about it?

That's what this week is. Don't let your diagnosis stay on paper.

steps.title: Emotional Safety: The Prerequisite for Repair
steps.body:
Before you can repair anything, both people need to feel safe.

Emotional safety means:
• I can tell you the truth without being punished for it
• I can express anger without you abandoning me
• I can be wrong without being made wrong

Without safety, 'repair' is just one person surrendering to keep the peace. That's not repair — that's appeasement.

Safety doesn't mean comfort. It means the relationship can handle hard conversations without collapsing.

questions.label:
On a scale of 1-5, how safe do you feel sharing a hard truth with your partner? (1=not safe at all, 5=completely safe)

What would need to be different for that number to be one point higher?

steps.question: Conflict is a sign that a marriage is in trouble.
feedback: Correct. Conflict is normal and inevitable. It's not a sign of trouble — it's a sign that things matter to both of you. The real danger isn't conflict; it's conflict without repair. Repair week is about making your conflicts productive instead of destructive.
Not quite. Conflict itself isn't the problem — every couple disagrees. The issue is what happens after. Do you recover? Do you grow? Do you learn? That's what repair skills make possible.

steps.title: What This Week Will Feel Like
steps.body:
Repair work is uncomfortable. That's normal. You might feel resistant, defensive, or like you're being judged.

Here's the reframe: the discomfort isn't punishment. It's the stretching feeling before growth.

This week you'll:
• Learn to speak hard truths without causing damage
• Practice listening without getting defensive
• Start mending one specific crack in your marriage
• Build the habit of turning toward instead of away

Some days will feel heavy. Some will feel hopeful. Both are progress.

steps.question: Emotional safety means:
options: You can express yourself without fear of punishment or abandonment
You always agree with each other
You never talk about hard things
feedback: Not quite. Emotional safety isn't about avoiding conflict — it's about knowing the relationship can handle hard conversations.
Right. Safety means you can be honest, angry, or wrong — and your relationship stays intact. That's the foundation for real repair.
Not quite. Safety doesn't mean agreement. It means disagreement doesn't threaten the relationship.
Not quite. Safety actually makes hard conversations possible, not impossible.

steps.title: Your Week 2 Starter
steps.prompt:
This week is about action. Start now.

Write down one thing you learned about your marriage in Week 1 that you want to address this week.

Then write one small, specific action you can take TODAY to begin that repair.

Example: 'I learned that I withdraw during conflict. Today, I'll stay present for one uncomfortable conversation instead of shutting down.'
steps.body:
This week is about action. Start now.

Write down one thing you learned about your marriage in Week 1 that you want to address this week.

Then write one small, specific action you can take TODAY to begin that repair.

Example: 'I learned that I withdraw during conflict. Today, I'll stay present for one uncomfortable conversation instead of shutting down.'

steps.title: Tomorrow: The Art of the Do-Over
steps.body:
Today you set the intention for Repair Week. You named what needs mending.

Tomorrow, you'll learn a specific skill that's more powerful than any apology: the do-over. How to pause a moment that's going wrong, reset, and actually have the conversation you need to have.

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
